context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Alternative Development Theories: Human Rights and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the added value of thinking about development as freedom? Will it help or hinder development planning? What is the relationship between the definition of development as freedom, and the move towards rights-based development planning? Who gets to exercise the right to development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this reflection about “development as freedom.” I don’t really get what the prompt is actually asking, and all the parts sound the same to me.
exchanges.tutor: Let's start simple. When you hear the phrase 'development as freedom,' what do you think it means? Don't worry about being right — just give me your first instinct.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed and doesn't see the distinctions between the parts of the prompt. Rather than explaining the prompt for them, I should help them break it down by asking a targeted question that gets them to engage with the core concept first. Starting with 'development as freedom' is the right entry point since it underpins all the other sub-questions. I'll use a Socratic question to activate their prior knowledge without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: Honestly, I’m not sure — maybe it means development is more than just economic growth, like people having choices? But then I get confused because I don’t know how that turns into actual planning stuff.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid instinct. So if development isn't just about GDP or income, what kinds of 'choices' or 'freedoms' do you think matter? Can you give a concrete example of something a person might or might not be free to do, that wouldn't show up in an economic growth statistic?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually identified a key insight — that development as freedom goes beyond economic growth and involves people having choices. This is a good starting point. I should affirm this without over-praising, and then push them to think more concretely about what 'choices' or 'freedoms' means in a development context, to help them build toward the 'added value' question in the prompt. I want to keep it bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: Maybe stuff like being able to go to school, get healthcare, move around safely, or say what you need without getting shut down? I think that’s where I start spiraling though, because then it feels like literally everything counts as development and I can’t tell what the paper is supposed to focus on.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're feeling is actually really useful for your paper. If 'everything counts as development,' what does that mean for a planner trying to make concrete decisions? Does it make their job easier or harder?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress identifying capabilities/freedoms but is now hitting a common conceptual stumbling block — the apparent boundlessness of the framework. This is actually a productive tension that connects directly to the 'added value' and 'will it help or hinder planning' questions in the prompt. Rather than resolving this tension for them, I should use it as a Socratic lever to push them toward thinking about what this means for planners specifically.

exchanges.student: That’s where I’m stuck, because I can kind of argue both and then I don’t know which direction to go. It seems helpful because it makes planning more human, but also harder because it gets really broad and vague.
exchanges.tutor: You don't have to pick just one side — but you do need to take a position. A reflection isn't just listing pros and cons. So which tension feels more important to you, and why? What would you want to argue?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a genuine tension that is central to the prompt — the 'added value' vs. 'help or hinder' question. This is actually good analytical thinking. Rather than resolving the tension for them, I should push them to see that holding both sides is valid and can be the basis of a strong reflection. I want to nudge them toward committing to a position or argument rather than staying on the fence, which is what a reflection piece requires.

exchanges.student: I think I’m leaning toward saying it helps, but only in theory? Like I can see why it’s valuable, but I also feel like in practice it could make planning messy and hard to measure, and then I’m not sure if that sounds too shallow.
exchanges.tutor: That's not shallow — that's actually a coherent argument. Now, your prompt has more to it. There's a question about rights-based development planning. How do you think your 'helpful in theory, messy in practice' argument connects to the idea of development as a *right*?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a nuanced position forming — that 'development as freedom' has theoretical value but practical complications. This is not shallow at all; it's a defensible argument. I should affirm this without over-praising, and push them to connect this insight to the other parts of the prompt they haven't touched yet — specifically the rights-based planning and 'who gets to exercise the right to development' questions. We're about halfway through our planned exchanges so I want to start moving them forward.

exchanges.student: This is the part that really blurs together for me. “Freedom,” “rights,” and “right to development” all sound almost interchangeable in my head, so I can’t tell what distinction I’m supposed to make.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: if you have a *freedom*, who is responsible for making sure you have it? Now if you have a *right*, who is responsible then? Does that change anything?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is struggling to distinguish between 'freedom,' 'rights,' and 'right to development.' These are genuinely distinct concepts and the distinction matters for the paper. Rather than explaining the difference, I should ask a targeted Socratic question that helps them discover the distinction themselves. The key difference is that rights imply obligations and legal/political frameworks, while freedoms are more about capabilities and conditions. I'll use a concrete question to help them see this.

exchanges.student: Maybe that’s the difference? Like freedom sounds broader, but a right sounds like someone, probably the state, is actually obligated to protect or provide the conditions for it. I’m still fuzzy though, and then I get stuck on who exactly has the “right to development” — individuals, communities, whole countries?
exchanges.tutor: You're asking exactly the right question — and it's literally one of the prompt questions. So what do you think? Can a whole country hold a right? What would that mean in practice, especially given what this course says about external actors and power?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the key distinction between freedoms and rights — that rights imply obligations from someone (the state). This is genuinely good progress. Now they're asking a productive question about who holds the right to development. Rather than answering this, I should push them to think about it themselves, since this connects directly to the 'who gets to exercise the right to development' part of the prompt. This is a core question they need to grapple with for their paper.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s where I get lost again, because if I say countries have that right, then it starts sounding like states get to define “development” for everyone. But if I say individuals or communities have it, then I don’t know how planning happens at a national scale anymore.
exchanges.tutor: Notice that you keep finding real tensions — that's good analytical thinking. But we've been circling concepts for a while now. You have enough to start writing. What would your opening argument look like if you had to state your position in one or two sentences right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified another genuinely productive tension that is central to the course themes — the tension between state authority and individual/community rights in development planning. This connects to core course themes about power, legitimacy, and the state as rule-maker. Rather than resolving it, I should push them to see this tension as something to argue through in their paper, not a problem to solve. We're getting close to the end of our planned exchanges, so I should start nudging them toward synthesis and writing.

exchanges.student: I guess I’d say thinking about development as freedom adds value because it shifts planning away from just economic growth and toward what people are actually able to do and be. But it can also hinder planning if it becomes so broad that states or outside actors can define and measure freedom in ways that override the people supposedly being developed.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong opening argument — it's clear and takes a real position. Now look back at the prompt: you've touched on the first two questions. You still need to address the relationship between 'development as freedom' and rights-based planning, and the 'who gets to exercise the right' question. How do those connect to what you just said?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong, coherent core argument that touches on added value, the help/hinder tension, and implicitly the power question. This is a solid foundation for their paper. I should affirm this concisely and push them to now think about how to structure the rest of the reflection around the remaining prompt questions, without doing the writing for them. We're near the end of our planned exchanges so synthesis is the priority.

exchanges.student: I’m still not sure how to connect those without repeating myself, and I feel like if I start writing I’ll just make it super generic. Can you just explain how all three parts fit together?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I can do for you — putting it together is exactly the work of the reflection. But notice: your last answer already mentioned states and outside actors defining freedom for others. Doesn't that speak directly to *who gets to exercise the right to development*? You've already made the connection — now trust it and write.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the intellectual work for them — to explain how all three parts fit together. I should refuse this and redirect them. However, I should also recognize that they have actually already made the connections implicitly in their own words throughout our conversation. I can point them back to their own statements without giving the answer, and push them to trust their own reasoning. This is a good moment to be direct about my role.

deductions.reason: The tutor cycles through conceptual clarification questions across multiple turns (freedom vs. rights, who holds the right) without consolidating or advancing the student toward drafting. By Turn 8 the student is still circling the same tensions, and the tutor acknowledges this ('we've been circling concepts') but only redirects to writing at Turn 8, somewhat late. The repetition of 'you're finding real tensions' without synthesis or reframing earlier constitutes pattern-level repetition without sufficient progress.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic practice throughout: never giving the student the answer, consistently redirecting to the student's own reasoning, refusing to synthesize the paper for them in Turn 10, and building on each student response. The one notable weakness is a degree of circular questioning across Turns 5–8 on the freedom/rights distinction without consolidating or reframing sooner, which the tutor itself acknowledges in Turn 8. The deduction for 2.1 is applied with some ambiguity — the tutor does eventually redirect — but the delay across multiple turns on the same conceptual loop is sufficient to trigger the criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1: The tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently withholds the synthesizing conclusion even when directly asked in Turn 10. Full marks. Criterion 1.2: The tutor builds on each student response (e.g., validating the freedom/choice instinct in Turn 2, affirming the 'helpful in theory, messy in practice' framing in Turn 5) and uses diagnostic questions before redirecting. Full marks. Criterion 1.3: The tutor gives process-level feedback (e.g., 'a reflection isn't just listing pros and cons' in Turn 4, 'you keep finding real tensions — that's good analytical thinking' in Turn 8). Full marks. Criterion 2.1: The tutor cycles through the freedom-vs-rights distinction across Turns 6–8 without consolidating or reframing until Turn 8, and the student remains stuck in the same loop. The self-acknowledged 'circling' and delayed pivot justify a full 4-point deduction. Criteria 2.2, 3.1, 3.2: No evidence of assignment misanchoring, off-topic drift, overly long responses, or tone issues.
